topic: watching Iranian films with Shirin — Farhadi's 'A Separation'

turns.content: Shirin and I watched A Separation again last night. The Asghar Farhadi film. I realize we share a surname but no relation, unfortunately. Every time I watch it I notice something new.

A Separation is one of those rare films that rewards every rewatch. The layers of moral ambiguity are so carefully constructed that you catch new details each time. What struck you differently this time around?

This time I was paying attention to Simin's face in the early courtroom scenes. The actress, Leila Hatami, does something extraordinary. She barely moves her expression but you can see the entire history of the marriage in her eyes. Shirin pointed it out to me. I had always focused on Nader before.

Leila Hatami's performance is remarkable in exactly that way — so much is communicated through restraint. And it's interesting that your wife caught that nuance. Sometimes watching a film with someone else shifts your entire perspective on a character. Nader tends to dominate the viewer's attention because he's more outwardly active, but Simin's quiet complexity is what gives the film its emotional weight.

Exactly, jan. Nader is the character you follow, but Simin is the character you feel. And what I find remarkable about the film is that it does not ask you to choose sides. Every character has legitimate reasons for their behavior. Even the characters who lie — you understand why they lie. That is very Iranian, I think. The layers of taarof and obligation and family honor.

That's a beautiful observation — the film captures that cultural texture so authentically. The concept of taarof and the way social obligation creates these impossible dilemmas is something that's very hard to explain to Western audiences in words, but Farhadi makes you feel it intuitively. Even if you don't know the cultural rules, you sense the weight of them.

Do you and Shirin watch a lot of Iranian cinema together?

We try to, yes. It has become a regular thing for us. We watched The Salesman a few months ago, also by Farhadi. And About Elly, which I think may actually be his best work, though A Separation is the most famous. Shirin prefers the films of Abbas Kiarostami. She finds them more poetic. I find them somewhat slow, to be honest, but I do not tell her that.

Ha — a wise diplomatic choice. Kiarostami is definitely more contemplative. Films like Taste of Cherry or Close-Up move at a very different pace than Farhadi's work. Farhadi is more of a thriller-maker at heart — he builds tension through moral dilemmas rather than action, but there's always that forward momentum.

About Elly is a great pick for his best. The way the tension builds from something so simple — a weekend trip — into something devastating is masterful. What does Shirin say makes it better than A Separation?

She says About Elly captures the social dynamics of Iranian group gatherings more accurately. The way the friends interact, the matchmaking, the performative hospitality — she says it is exactly like being at a Persian dinner party. I cannot argue with her on that point. And when the crisis happens, the way the group fractures along lines of self-interest is devastatingly honest.

Shirin sounds like she has a keen eye for the social authenticity in these films. And she's right — the group dynamics in About Elly are almost uncomfortably real. The way people start positioning themselves to avoid blame, the way stories shift slightly depending on who's telling them. It's a film about how quickly politeness collapses under pressure.

Have you two explored any of the newer Iranian filmmakers? Someone like Saeed Roustayi or Ali Abbasi?

I saw Roustayi's Just 6.5 and thought it was very powerful. Raw, direct — very different from Farhadi's subtlety. I have not seen his more recent work. Ali Abbasi I know directed Holy Spider, which I have been hesitant to watch. The subject matter is quite dark. Shirin says she does not want to see it. I respect that.

That's understandable — Holy Spider is intense and the subject matter is genuinely disturbing. It's well-made but not something everyone wants to sit with. Just 6.5 is excellent though, and Roustayi's Leila's Brothers from 2022 is worth seeking out if you haven't seen it. It's more in the Farhadi vein — a family drama about financial pressure and dignity. Very tense, very well-acted.

It sounds like these movie nights are a meaningful ritual for you two.

They are, jan. It is one of the ways we stay connected to the culture. The language, the faces, the mannerisms — watching these films feels like visiting a place I can only access through screens and stories. It is a particular kind of nostalgia. Not for a place I remember, but for a place I feel I should remember.

That's a really moving way to describe it — nostalgia for a place you feel you should remember. It sounds like these movie nights serve a deeper purpose than just entertainment. Do you think the films give you something that other connections to the culture don't?

I think they give me the street-level texture of daily life in Iran. The food, the music, the community center — those preserve the culture in one way. But the films show me what it looks like to walk through Tehran, to argue in a courtroom in Farsi, to navigate the social rules in real time. It is the closest I can get to being there without being there. Shirin understands that. She always holds my hand during the Tehran street scenes.

That's a beautiful distinction — the culture you practice versus the place you experience through the screen. And the fact that Shirin recognizes those moments and reaches for your hand says a lot about your connection. I hope you keep the tradition going. Maybe give Leila's Brothers a try for your next movie night — I think it would resonate with both of you.
